title: New project assistants onboard at DTCC
abstract: DTCC has been reinforced by two new project assistants. By engaging directly with municipalities and end users, Ammar Kasem and Nuri Sechkin are set to help shape how digital twin tools create value in practice.
summary: DTCC has been reinforced by two new project assistants. By engaging directly with municipalities and end users, Ammar Kasem and Nuri Sechkin are set to help shape how digital twin tools create value in practice.
intro: DTCC has been reinforced by two new project assistants. By engaging directly with municipalities and end users, Ammar Kasem and Nuri Sechkin are set to help shape how digital twin tools create value in practice.
body:
Recently, Ammar Kasem and Nuri Sechkin joined the Digital Twin Cities Centre as project assistants. The focus of their work will be strengthening the connection between technical development and real-world application, by for example engaging municipalities and stakeholders to better understand current challenges.

“It’s exciting to be a part of DTCC. Right now, we are contacting municipalities and collecting feedback so developers can build according to real user stories. That way, development becomes more purposeful. We see ourselves as a bridge between DTCC and the municipalities, where we can help translate needs into concrete actions and support real case projects. Instead of just presenting tools, we want to help them achieve what they actually need and bring that feedback back into the system,” says Ammar Kasem, project assistant at DTCC.

This user-focused approach is expected to play an important role in addressing challenges such as data management, technical capabilities, and how digital tools can be implemented in everyday municipal workflows. By working closely with end users early on, the project assistants contribute to making development more targeted and relevant, while also identifying gaps and opportunities that might otherwise be overlooked.

“We are still early in the process, but the goal is to understand both the challenges and the potential. It’s about identifying what is missing, what could be improved, and how engagement can be expanded beyond just the technical stakeholders. There is also an opportunity to involve a wider audience over time, including citizens, to make these tools more accessible and meaningful,” says Nuri Sechkin, project assistant in DTCC.

**Complementary backgrounds with a shared focus**

Ammar and Nuri bring different but complementary perspectives into DTCC, reflecting the centre’s interdisciplinary approach. With a background in structural engineering combined with experience in IT and digitalization within the construction sector, Ammar Kasem has worked with BIM, automation, and data-driven workflows.

Nuri Sechkin comes from architecture and urban design, with experience ranging from housing projects to large-scale urban planning and a strong interest in digital tools for spatial design. Together, their combined expertise strengthens DTCC’s ability to connect technical development with spatial and societal perspectives.

Both Ammar and Nuri see digital twins as a key enabler for more informed and sustainable decision-making, particularly when it comes to understanding complex systems at scale.
“If you can see the bigger picture through a digital twin, your decisions will be much better in the long term. There is also a strong potential for circularity, where materials and resources can be reused more efficiently across a city. With better data and better visibility, you can optimize both costs and sustainability and even automate parts of how cities operate through connected systems,” says Ammar Kasem.

For Nuri Sechkin, the value also lies in capturing how cities evolve over time and enabling a deeper understanding of urban dynamics.

“One of the most important aspects is the temporal dimension. Today, we often work with a static image of a city, but digital twins allow us to understand how spaces change and evolve over time. That makes it possible to engage with deeper layers of urban environments and design in a way that reflects real-life dynamics. That potential is extremely valuable when thinking about the future of cities,” says Nuri Sechkin.
